content.title: Genetics in General Biology
content.description: Welcome to this lesson on genetics, a key area in biology that explains how traits are passed from one generation to the next, how genetic information is expressed, and how we can manipulate genes for various applications. This lesson explores four important subtopics: Mendel&#039;s Law of Inheritance, Sex Linkage, the Central Dogma of Molecular Biology, and Recombinant DNA technology.

video.description: This video provides an overview of Genetics.

subtopic.title: Mendel's Law of Inheritance
subtopic.paragraph: Gregor Mendel, often called the "Father of Genetics," conducted experiments on pea plants that laid the foundation for our understanding of inheritance. Mendel’s work led to the development of two key laws:

This law states that each individual has two alleles for each trait (one from each parent), and these alleles separate during the formation of gametes (eggs or sperm), so each gamete carries only one allele for each trait. When fertilization occurs, the offspring inherits one allele from each parent, creating a pair of alleles that determine the trait.

This law explains that alleles for different traits segregate independently of one another during gamete formation. This means that the inheritance of one trait (like flower color) does not influence the inheritance of another (like plant height), assuming the genes for these traits are on different chromosomes.
Together, these laws form the basis of how traits are inherited across generations and explain patterns of dominant and recessive traits.

subtopic.title: Sex Linkage
subtopic.paragraph: Sex linkage refers to genes that are located on the sex chromosomes (X and Y in humans). These genes are inherited differently depending on whether they are on the X or Y chromosome. In humans, females have two X chromosomes (XX), and males have one X and one Y chromosome (XY).

A well-known example of X-linked inheritance is color blindness. The gene for color vision is located on the X chromosome. Since males have only one X chromosome, if they inherit a defective color vision gene, they will be colorblind. Females, however, have two X chromosomes, so even if they inherit one defective gene, they may still have normal color vision if the other X chromosome carries the normal gene. This is why X-linked disorders are more common in males than females.

subtopic.title: Central Dogma of Molecular Biology
subtopic.paragraph: The Central Dogma of Molecular Biology describes the flow of genetic information within a biological system. It is the process by which the instructions in DNA are converted into functional products, usually proteins. The central dogma involves two key processes:

The genetic information in DNA is copied into mRNA (messenger RNA). This happens in the nucleus of the cell. The mRNA then carries the genetic message from the DNA out of the nucleus and into the cytoplasm.

The mRNA is used as a template to assemble a chain of amino acids, which will fold into a protein. This occurs at the ribosome in the cytoplasm. Proteins are the molecules that perform many of the functions in a cell, from building structures to regulating biochemical reactions.
The central dogma is often summarized as DNA → RNA → Protein, showing the directional flow of genetic information.

subtopic.title: Recombinant DNA Technology
subtopic.paragraph: Recombinant DNA technology is a set of techniques used to manipulate DNA and introduce new genes into an organism. This technology allows scientists to "recombine" DNA from different sources, creating new genetic combinations that are not found in nature. It has a wide range of applications in medicine, agriculture, and research.
The basic steps in recombinant DNA technology include:
A gene of interest (for example, the gene for insulin) is isolated from the DNA of an organism.
The gene is then inserted into a DNA molecule called a vector (often a plasmid, which is a small circular piece of DNA from bacteria).
The recombinant DNA is inserted into a host cell (often bacteria), which will reproduce and make copies of the gene.

The host organism will then produce the protein coded by the inserted gene. For example, bacteria engineered to carry the human insulin gene can produce insulin, which is harvested and used for medical purposes.
Recombinant DNA technology has revolutionized fields such as genetic engineering, allowing for the production of hormones, vaccines, genetically modified crops, and much more.

question.text: What does the Law of Segregation state?
question.option: Alleles for different traits segregate independently during gamete formation.
Genes located on different chromosomes segregate independently.
Traits are inherited in pairs, with one allele from each parent.
Each individual has two alleles for each trait, which separate during gamete formation.

question.text: Why are X-linked disorders more common in males than in females?
question.option: Males have two X chromosomes, so they are more likely to inherit the defective gene.
Males have only one X chromosome, so they express X-linked traits more easily.
Females are less likely to inherit the X chromosome from their mother.
X-linked genes only affect males because of the Y chromosome.

question.text: Which process in the Central Dogma converts DNA into mRNA?
question.option: Translation
Transcription
Replication
Protein synthesis

question.text: What is the primary purpose of recombinant DNA technology?
question.option: To alter the genetic makeup of organisms by inserting foreign genes.
To clone genes for medical treatments.
To isolate genes for analysis.
To repair damaged DNA.

question.text: What type of gene is commonly inserted into bacteria for the production of insulin in recombinant DNA technology?
question.option: A gene for antibiotic resistance
A gene for human growth hormone
A gene for insulin
A gene for ribosomal RNA
